turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument first this morning in Case 13짯433, Integrity Staffing Solutions v. Busk. Mr. Clement.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Going through security as part of the egress process is a classic postliminary activity that is non짯compensable under the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. It is materially similar to the process of checking out at the end of the day or waiting in line to do so, which is a quintessential postliminary activity under the Act. The Ninth Circuit erred in treating this time as integral and indispensable to a principal activity and Respondent's position that the time is compensable without regard to whether it is an integral or indispensable activity is more problematic still.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Clement 짯짯 excuse me 짯짯 can I give you a hypo? Suppose that there's an employer with cash registers, and there's a very extensive process for closing out the cash registers, and that extensive process is to protect against theft. If it weren't for that concern, you could close out a cash register much more quickly. Or the same for bank tellers or the same for casino dealers, you know, that there's, like, a 20짯minute process which is essentially an antitheft security process. And it happens at the end of the shift when the cashier goes off duty. What's the difference between that case and going through security in 짯짯 at Amazon?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I think one difference is not 짯짯 I'm not crystal clear that that time would not be non짯compensable because I think that's sort of the winding down period, which is, I think, with 짯짯 at least potentially within the ambit of preliminary and postliminary activities.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Do you know, by the way? I mean, before you get on to that, do you know how that's treated under the law? Because I guess my assumption was that this kind of period would be treated as compensable. But if that's not right, let me know.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, it's a problem with hypotheticals because I don't know that that particular case has arisen. I would think that would be actually a close question under the Act, because you do have this notion under the Portal짯to짯Portal Act that preliminary activities and postliminary activities are non짯compensable. And that, if you go back to Mt. Clemens, which used the term "preliminary," that included the sort of wind up process and might well include this sort of wind down process at the work station.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Couldn't you say that closing down the cash register is part of the job?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: You could.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: But getting yourself inspected as you leave 짯짯 as you leave the place of business is not part of the job. The other case where it's just part of the job.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: You could definitely say that, Justice Scalia, and I meant to get that 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: You could not only say it; it seems to me true.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, all the better, then, to say it, which is that this then becomes an easier case than that because it is part of the egress process, which is really the process of getting from your 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: But part of Justice Kagan's hypothetical was that the 20 minutes or the 30 minutes is just for antitheft purposes. Or at least I'll interpret her hypothetical that way. Just for antitheft purposes. Otherwise the cashiers 짯짯 the records and so forth are turned up. But just for antitheft protection, you need the 20 or 30 minutes. If I can interpret the hypothetical that way, then it seems to me to be the same.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I don't think it's the same. I think it's an arguable case under the Act that it still might be postliminary, but I think if you look at the cases that are out there, the courts have struggled a bit with the preliminary and postliminary activities that take place at the work station. Where I don't think they've struggled until the Ninth Circuit decision here is when you're talking about time after you've left the primary work station and when you're in the process of going to the doors.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That would seem to make it depend on a complete fortuity. You know, if I 짯짯 if I have the cashier walk from the 짯짯 the 짯짯 with her tray to the manager's station and do the same thing there on the way out the door, there would be one answer, and if she does it at the cashier's station, it would be another answer. And that seems not particularly sensible.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, there are some not particularly sensible results under the Portal짯to짯Portal Act because things do turn on where activities take place vis짯횪짯vis walking time. But I do think what makes this an easier case than your hypothetical is the exit screenings are a logical part of the egress process. The other side in this case tries to raise the specter of lawn mowing being uncompensated and car washing being uncompensated, but no employer requires to you take a couple of swipes at a car on your way out the door.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Mr. Clement, is it 짯짯 is it irrelevant that we're told here that because there are not enough security checkers and because all the shifts get out at the same time, what could be a five짯minute process turns out to be 25 minutes, 25 minutes of the workers' time, and 20 of those allegedly would not occur if the employer had 짯짯 provided sufficient staffing.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Justice Ginsburg, ultimately, we don't think that allegation is relevant here. Now, it is just an allegation. But the reason that I say that it's not relevant here is twofold. One, the one thing we know from the Portal짯to짯Portal Act and the pre짯Portal짯to짯Portal Act cases is the pure length of time of something does not take it out of preliminary or postliminary activities. If you go back to the Jewell Ridge case and look at Justice Jackson's dissent, he makes crystal clear that the travel time at issue there was over an hour total, and nonetheless the clear import of Portal짯to짯Portal Act is to treat that hour as non짯compensable time. The second reason why I think this waiting time at the exit would be particularly a bad candidate for treating as compensable just because it was relatively long is because it's not uniform 25 minutes. And so it's directly analogous to the time that was 짯짯 people had to wait to go in and punch the clock at the pottery factory in Mt. Clemens. And even the Mt. Clemens Court didn't treat the waiting time to punch in as compensable. And I think part of the reason was that it didn't really make any sense. It took eight minutes for everybody to get through, but that would create all sorts of anomalies where the first person who got through would get treated 짯짯 would get paid for eight more minutes than the last person that got through.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But that's true 짯짯 I'm sorry. That's true of a workday generally. Some people have to close down the shop; others don't. So it takes the people who are closing down the shop longer. I'm not worried about 짯짯

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Could I stop you there, though? I think that's not really true of the compensable principal time of the workday after the Portal짯to짯Portal Act, which is whistle to whistle.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Yeah, but could we start 짯짯 could we start with that question?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Sure.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: What's a principal activity? How is it defined? Because pre or post짯activity to a principal activity, you still have to define what a principal activity is. And 짯짯 and so I think, isn't a principal activity work that benefits the employer in some way?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: No, Justice Sotomayor. And I think it's important to get two concepts separate. One is work under the Fair Labor Standards Act. And for purposes of that, all you really do need is things that are required by the employer for the employer's benefit that require a minimum amount of exertion. That's the test from this Court's cases. But principal activities is a separate and more demanding test under the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. And the way I'd think about the statute is the Fair Labor Standards Act makes all work presumptively compensable. And then only when you get to something that is arguably postliminary or preliminary do you have to ask a question that involves principal activities. Because if you have compensable work and it's not even arguably preliminary or postliminary, it's compensable without regard to the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. But when you get to the Portal짯to짯Portal Act, then you have to look, is this a preliminary and postliminary activity. And if it is, then it's presumptively noncompensable unless it's integral and indispensable to a principal activity.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, let's 짯짯 let's take Justice Ginsburg's hypothetical. Let's 짯짯 let's assume that it takes 25 minutes to check out and that it would be very easy for the employer to hire a few more checkers and make it 5 minutes. Just assume that that's the fact. Why isn't the long line in 짯짯 caused by very few checkers for the benefit of the employer? It's for the benefit of the employer to hire fewer checkers.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: It might be for the benefit of the employer in that set of circumstances, Justice Kennedy, but that doesn't make it not postliminary activity and not compensable. And if you look at the universe of all noncompensable time under the Portal짯to짯Portal Act, I think in every instance you could say if the employer only did more, he could reduce 짯짯 he or she, it could reduce that time. And so, if you go back 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: He could move his plant, for example, to be closer to the city where the employees live, right? So it takes him an hour to get to work. It's his fault that it 짯짯 that it takes them an hour instead of just 10 minutes. He should move his plant. It's just a matter of cost, right?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: That's exactly right, Justice Scalia. And you can ticket from the commuting time to the commuting time internally if you go back to those trams in the 짯짯 in the coal mining cases. If the employer added more trams or more tram drivers, presumably, that travel time would be reduced.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Is it 짯짯 is it always irrelevant to the analysis that it's for or not for the benefit of the employer?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: It is irrelevant for the Portal짯to짯Portal Act analysis. And as I said, it's not irrelevant to the analysis at all because the fact that it is for the employer's benefit is part of what makes it compensable work presumptively or work under the Fair Labor Standards Act. But when you get to applying the Portal짯to짯Portal Act, that is activity that even though it's work, even though it's required by the employer, and even though it's for the benefit of the employer, is nonetheless noncompensable by the terms of the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. And if I could just continue the 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. That's 짯짯 that's not quite right, because if the employer requires you to put on a particular outfit that you can't do the work without, we've said that's compensable.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I don't think you have a case 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So 짯짯 and 짯짯 but donning generally isn't, so I'm not sure quite how you can take out that element from this analysis.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor, I don't think there's a case of this Court that says just because the employer requires something that would otherwise be superfluous that it therefore becomes integral and indispensable. And so I would actually take 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: I agree with you. It can't be superfluous. It can't be something that's 짯짯 for the benefit of the employee. Putting on clothes to keep yourself from being splattered with a nonharmful substance is for the benefit of the employee. But 짯짯

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: But 짯짯 but take something where a particular employer has an idiosyncratic view and actually wants their employers 짯짯 employees to have a certain color smock or a certain piece of equipment that's not actually particularly integral and indispensable to anything. I don't think anything in this Court's cases would say just because the employer required it, it is therefore compensable. And I think if you go back to the quintessential example of something that is postliminary and noncompensable, the time clocks, the checking out process, all of that is required by the employer and for the employer's benefit. And so I don't think you can meaningfully distinguish the exit screening from those quintessentially postliminary noncompensable things.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Where did that come from? Indispensable. Indispensable. Hardly anything is indispensable. Where 짯짯 where does that word come from?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, the word comes from this Court's decision in Steiner and it come 짯짯 comes from the Labor Department's 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The Labor Department says 짯짯 they're here saying it's the correct test, which I think is 짯짯 well, perhaps more important, is there a close and direct relationship? Which I understand a little bit better. Isn't that labor?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: That's their gloss on what integral and indispensable means.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Oh, I see. Okay.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: And what I 짯짯 what I would say 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Well, I mean, it can't literally mean indispensable, can it? Because then anything at the end of the day, they're probably five ways of doing it. The cash registers, you can do it this way. You can do it that way. I mean 짯짯

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I 짯짯 with all due respect, Justice Breyer, I do think that the test should be indispensable. And I do think there are plenty of things 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: If I can think of a way at the end of the day that the employer could have done it by saving a little more time or something like that, then it's postliminary.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, if it's otherwise postliminary, it doesn't come outside of that exception and become a principal activity. And I think two points to make here. One is, you know, I think the knife sharpening in King Packing, for example, really is indispensable. You can't run a butchering operation without sharp knives. I think you can perfectly well run a warehouse facility without egress security. So I think these are different.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Actually, Amazon, I don't think you can. I mean, what makes Amazon, Amazon? It's a system of inventory control that betters everybody else in the business. And what's really important to Amazon is that it knows where every toothbrush in the warehouse is. And that's just as integral to what Amazon does and to what it requires its employees to do as, for example, the 짯짯 I'm going back to my hypos 짯짯 but the person who closes out the cash register, the person who closes out the bank teller operation, is that this is sort of a necessary part of what 짯짯 of what the 짯짯 the folks who do all the stocking and the unshelving and shelving do at Amazon.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, I guess I would beg to differ, Justice Kagan. I think, certainly, everything in the Amazon facility is barcoded and the like, and everybody knows where everything is, but there's always the possibility that somebody would not barcode an incoming item at all and put it in their pocket. Now, if they're doing that, they're not discharging their principal activities, and if they're detected on the way out, I mean, that might help keep the next person on mission, but that doesn't make it integral and indispensable to discharging the primary job duty.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I suppose that it is also necessary to Amazon's business that it know how many hours each of its workers has worked, so it knows how much to pay them and doesn't pay them more, right? And 짯짯 and yet, there's no doubt whatever, is there, that punching in and punching out is not 짯짯 is 짯짯 is preliminary and postliminary, right?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I hope there's no doubt about that, Justice Scalia. And I do think that the exit security screenings are just the modern 짯짯 modern analogue of that. Not only do they both come at the employer's insistence and for the employer's benefit, but they also have this process of verifying that the employers are essentially behaving in an honest and 짯짯 and 짯짯 way. It's the same way as trying to ensure that an employee is not lying about having worked an eight짯hour day. You use the time clocks to verify that. You also use the exit screening for that process. And the other aspect of it that makes it so closely analogous is they are a logical part of the egress process. They are part of getting you from your principal work station to the exit doors at the end of the day, classically postliminary activity. If that's not 짯짯 if the Court is to treat that as compensable, then it's not clear what's left of the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. The Portal짯to짯Portal Act was Congress's judgment that these kind of preliminary and postliminary activities should not be compensable. It would be perfectly rational to have a system where you compensated employees from portal to portal, and that's basically the regime that this Court created in a series of cases culminating in Mt. Clemens Pottery. But it's just as clear that Congress reversed that result in Portal짯to짯Portal Act. If I could reserve the balance of my time.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Gannon. AS AMICUS CURIAE, SUPPORTING PETITIONER

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The Portal짯to짯Portal Act generally accepted from mandatory compensation the activities that are associated with a process by which employees arrive on the employer's premises at the entrance to the portal and get to the place where they perform their principal work activities at the beginning of the day, and then the process by which they leave at the end of the day and get to the exits. We think that security screenings here are noncompensable because they are postliminary activities, and they are not integral and indispensable to the employees' principal work activities.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You said in your brief that there are some security searches that would be compensable. So would you tell us which ones are and which ones aren't?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Well, I think you're referring to footnote 18 of our brief, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: And there we talk about the fact that the employer can't just require lots of other things to be done at the end of the day and call those things postliminary. I don't think that we have any particular examples of searches in mind. There aren't cases that deal with compensable searches. But I think the question there would be whether the activity has so fundamentally changed the nature of what's going on that it no longer resembles the ordinary process of checking out. The reason why we think 짯짯

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: You can't think of an example, then, where a security check, an exit security check, would be compensable?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Well, I think it would probably have to be a type of search that is so dramatically different, more intrusive, more time짯consuming, the search itself, not just the time waiting in line, that it's fundamentally transformed the nature of the activity. So I think to the extent that some of the examples that are suggested in the briefs, like drug testing, you could analogize that in some way to an anti짯theft search. One of the hypotheticals has to do with a person who works in a pharmaceutical manufacturing facility, have they taken the pharmaceuticals. The employer may want to test them on the way out. And I'd say that drug testing is not the type of thing that is normally associated with entering and leaving the property. It's not the ordinary course of checking in and checking out, waiting in line to do so, the types of things that the Labor Department was thinking about in 1947 when it adopted the interpretive regulation that gave those examples. And I also think that drug testing is not the sort of thing that's classically associated with the entrances and the exits. It's the sort of thing that is usually done somewhere else. And that's different from security and safety screenings, the most of which I understand Respondents concede are not actually going to be compensable. And so we think that those are the two reasons why in this case this looks like something that is both postliminary 짯짯 it happens as part of a process of getting out, it is 짯짯 it happens at the door, at the portal or near there 짯짯 and it is not integral and indispensable. It is different from the activities that were at issue in Steiner or in King Packing or in IBP, because the employees when they are on the work floor doing their job, surely they do have access to merchandise. It is of course important to Amazon, as you pointed out, Justice Kagan, that they not take the merchandise. Amazon and Integrity Staffing want to know where the merchandise is. But the idea that this benefits the employer or is required by the employer isn't enough to make it compensable because, as Mr. Clement was just saying, that's the test for whether something was work. That's what was the test under Mt. Clemens, and Congress excepted from that a class of activities, preliminary and postliminary activities, that are noncompensable. Travel time and preliminary and postliminary activities like time clocks, punching in and punching out, that's required by the employer. It benefits the employer. That's not enough to make it compensable.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But is the idea, then, Mr. Gannon, just 짯짯 you know, it's basically a door test? I mean, portal to portal. It might make sense just to have a door test, exits and entrances.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: I think that it has 짯짯 to be more precise about it, I think that canonically most of the activities are going to be associated with the process of getting in and out. Those were the 짯짯 those were the things that were at issue in the Portal짯to짯Portal Act cases that Congress was effectively reversing when it adopted the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. And I think it's the continuous workday rule that's going to make it 짯짯it makes sure that it happens only at the beginning and the end of the day. I think that, to be more precise about it, though, the way the statute is phrased, it talks about these being activities that occur before the principal work activities begin at the end of the day or after they cease. Something isn't preliminary if you've already started doing your principal activities and it's not postliminary if you haven't already ceased them. And so the question is whether this is itself a principal activity by virtue of being integral and indispensable and 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: Can we write this case without discussing whether or not this is for the benefit of the employer? Or do we have to address that?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Well, I think that it's always going to be for the benefit of the employer if it comes up in the context of the Portal짯to짯Portal Act, because we wouldn't be concerned about whether the exception 짯짯

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So you're saying that that is really not at all helpful or necessary for the analysis?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: I don't think it is, because that's the antecedent question about whether it would have been compensable under the FLSA. If it doesn't benefit the employer, if it's not required by the employer, it wouldn't have been work, you don't need to decide whether it's preliminary and postliminary and therefore excepted from the mandatory compensation requirement.

turns.name: ANTHONY KENNEDY
turns.text: So you can 짯짯 so we assume that it's for the benefit of the employer.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Yes. I think that it almost certainly is always going to be for the benefit of the employer, just like time clocks are, just like you could say the requirement in Tennessee Coal and Jewell Mining, the mine cases, that employees were required to ride from the face 짯짯 from the portal of the mine, down to the face where they are going to be working, they were required to ride in the ore skiff 짯짯 in the ore skip, and presumably that benefitted the employer for all sorts of reasons. They didn't have to make more room for people to walk. They didn't have to worry about employees getting injured by skips that were going by. And that's not enough to ensure 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Can I give you a different hypo, which is similar to some of the ones that have been floating around in the brief, but it's actually based on real life circumstances. There was a judge ages ago in the Southern District of New York who had his clerks 짯짯 all that they did was help him with his opinions and his cases and that was their principal activity, but had his clerks come early in order to cut his grapefruit and otherwise make breakfast for him. And would that be compensable?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Well, setting aside the question of whether the law clerks were covered by the FLSA to begin with 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: 짯짯 I take the point that that would be compensable, and I think there we're talking about a kind of activity that isn't preliminary or postliminary. We do think that those words have independent meaning. As we discuss in our brief 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: The one question I have for your side is this. I would 짯짯 perhaps no one else would, but I a pay a lot of attention to the Labor Department.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: So do we, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Yes, I understand that. But this is a dismissal of a complaint and it seems to me normally what happens is you debate these facts on the summary judgment. I don't know if they want to introduce something else. I mean, in my own mind, I can think of five things I'd like to know about the activity and about other activities and, you know, is it more like the cash register, is it more like this or that. So shouldn't we send it back so that if they want to develop the record further, they can?

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: I think, as the facts are alleged here, we know enough to know that these activities are not 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: No matter what? I mean, suppose it turns out that the warehouse thing 짯짯 you look all over the country and this kind of warehouse employee, of course they have security checks and they have a special kind of security checks, other people don't have them. This is just normal that a warehouse employee does have a security check at the end of the day, and, boy, it begins to look a little bit more integral. It begins to look like part of the job, just as he has to put the books back on the shelf. He has to put the books back on the shelf and he has to get a security check.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Putting books back on the shelf, closing out the cash register, taking the cash car to the supervisor, all of those things we think are compensable. Those are not 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: Then what's different about this? What you do is you put it back on the shelf, you go get your security checked, and there we are.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: Because those are still the activities that the employee is paying you to perform, and so that is the job that you are doing. You're still handling the money, you're still 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I guess I just don't understand that, Mr. Gannon, because, as Justice Kennedy said, the point of my hypo was that it's not part of the job. It's actually an antitheft mechanism.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: But it's part of the job because it's things that have to be done in order to 짯짯 I mean, the employer has set up the procedure in order to manage the cash. The cash has to be taken somewhere at the end of the day. When you're doing all of that 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: The cash could be taken somewhere much more quickly and much more easily if the employer were not worried about the employee pocketing some of it, you know. I mean, you could just put it in a big bag, versus going through this very, very careful sort of inventory control.

turns.name: CURTIS E. GANNON
turns.text: I don't think the purpose is enough to distinguish. And we discuss at the end of our brief about how there are safety and security searches, that the purposes overlap a lot here, whether the employer is trying to protect its time when it requires somebody to do punching in or punching out or to protect its property. I think the purpose is very difficult to make that the distinguishing factor. But there we think that those activities are still what the employer is paying you to do. They're not 짯짯 when you're done with the cash drawer, when you've turned in the cash and then you're done, you've ceased your principal activities for the day, now you need to walk out the door. And if at the door, they want to look in your purse, we think that that doesn't transform the time it took you from being done with your job to getting off the property into being compensable any more than it would with the donning and doffing cases. Once you had finished taking off the garb, you were done, and then if you still had to walk you weren't getting paid. In IBP, this Court held that when you were waiting in line to put on the equipment in the morning, if the employer decides that they're just a Nervous Nellie and they want you to be wearing all sort of extra security equipment that nobody really thinks is important but the employer demands that you do, and they think it benefits them, then you're going to have to get paid for the time it takes to put that on, but not the time you are waiting in line to do so.

Having said it's work, the question then becomes, is it a principal activity. Not whether it is integral and indispensable, but is it a principal activity, because we never get to integral and indispensable, we never get to postliminary and preliminary, if it is a standalone or a principal activity. And to answer that question 짯짯

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN

And no, they are mostly non짯union companies. You are talking about 3.0, and the clothes changing is a separate exception. But in fact, the reg on that says if it was required by law or the employer and it's not covered by the union contract, then it is compensable. So it is the requirement that the employer tell you to do it that, I think is the linchpin to make something a principal activity. If the employer didn't have a mandate, if the employer says to Justice Scalia, well, I don't really care how you get here but you better show up at 8 o'clock, you can get here at 7:30, you can get here at 7:45. turns.text: It's covered by a specific exception under the 254(a)(1). It's the travel. Mt. Clemens was the furthest extension of work we have. They rolled back Mt. Clemens. They rolled back a part of Tennessee Coal, which was the travel time, but when they rolled back Mt. Clemens, they only rolled back a portion of it. They rolled back the going from the portal to your work station part of it. They didn't roll back everything that was in Mt. Clemens. Even though it's in the briefs, I'm sure the Petitioner would not want to the de minimus rule to be eviscerated. It is a rule for their favor. That's in Mt. Clemens. There's lots of stuff in Mt. Clemens that still survives on both sides. But Mt. Clemens rolled back 짯짯 was rolled back by the Portal짯to짯Portal Act by the concept of you're on the property, you're in the employ. I mean, where 짯짯 Nevada has one of the 짯짯 is 짯짯 actually has that kind of sense of employment as opposed to the Federal employment, which is a much more employee 짯짯 employer directed. You do what you're told. Your day starts when your first command comes down. Here's 짯짯 the last command is to be searched. And it doesn't take 2 minutes; it takes 20 minutes, because they don't want to open up more kiosks and 짯짯 and 짯짯 or use some kind of queueing theory and shorten the lines. That's their choice. It's also a way of enforcing a 짯짯 a policy or a way of 짯짯 there are other ways to make sure people don't steal things. They could use cameras. They could use 짯짯 they could use a tally. They could make people stand by their bench at the end of the day and they go through every bin. Certainly that would be compensable. It's 짯짯 the fact is they chose this method. It's an employer's choice to do it, because it's the cheapest method to do it, to discourage employee theft. And if the merchandise isn't there at the 짯짯 at the end of the day or the beginning of the next shift, you can't do your job. So in that sense, it is indispensable. You need that merchandise not to be stolen so you can fill your orders. But just the command itself, unless it's a carveout, which we have a carveout for changing 짯짯 for checking in, checking out. We have a carveout for travel, community짯type travel. It's work because you're told to do it and it's not an employee's position to say, gee, I don't think this is work or I don't think this is compensable. They used the example of sneaking out the back door. We don't want employees to be going, oh, well, I'm not getting paid for this, so I'm going to sneak out the back door. Now, when it's a non짯mandated 짯짯 employer non짯mandated task, then we have a whole set of different rules. That's when we use integral and indispensable. Then we say: Is it necessary? Is it indispensable? Was he doing it for his own convenience or was he doing it for the employer's because he couldn't do the job without it? If we have a situation where we're 짯짯 we're told to do it and he doesn't do it, he's fired, I think that's 짯짯 that's ipso facto indispensable to his job, whether it makes sense or not from the employer's point of view. I mean, employers make people do all kinds of things that don't make sense. Anyway, the fact is that there are lots of examples in the regs of things that are just not 짯짯 that are not standalone jobs that are principal activities. And if the 짯짯 if the definition of a principal activity requires it to be related to something else 짯짯

You don't make grapefruit all day long for the judge. There are 짯짯 so the concept that it has to be a job in and of itself that you can have somebody else to do it is wrong. The changing clothes, the engage to wait. If you look in the 짯짯 if you look in the legislative history and you look in the regulations, there are two scenarios that Senator Cooper says explains everything. The first one is waiting 짯짯 is you've got to come in and oil your machine before you can do your job. That's integral and indispensable. But the second one is laying out work for other people. Now, we don't even know what that particular person's job is. For all we know, they could be the coffee maker or the 짯짯 or the gofer or whatever, but they have a task that has been assigned by their employer that they must be paid for doing, the laying out the work of other people. That's not integral and indispensable to their job. But it's a specific task. It's a principal activity. And that's 짯짯 and that's what I think the legislation contemplates.

So they escort you to the bathroom, they make somebody sit there and watch you while you 짯짯 while you 짯짯 while you give them a sample and then they go test it, and they won't let you leave until you're done testing it 짯짯 until they're done testing it and they have a result. That's a close example. And yet 짯짯

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN
turns.text: Waiting to put off, yes. See, we're 짯짯 we're dealing with a continuous workday rule and we're on the other end of it. Most of the cases everyone is talking about are preliminary to work. We're talking postliminary if 짯짯 or principal activity. And on the way out, it's the continuous workday rule works in favor of the employee. On the way in, it works in favor of the employer because the clock hasn't started. Anyway 짯짯

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All of the examples you've just been giving and what you say the rules are 짯짯

turns.text: Yes. They're in 짯짯 they're in the 짯짯 what the 짯짯 what the government and Petitioner don't discuss is the 785 series. The 785 series is what's work. The 785 series says that if you are required to take a 짯짯 a seminar, even if it's nothing to do with what you 짯짯 what you are employed to do, it's compensable. If it's a voluntary seminar, then it 짯짯 then there's a 짯짯 a kind of a test. Well, is it related to your work, not related to your work, is it going to advance your work or not advance it. But if it's totally unrelated to your work, but you're required to take the seminar, whether it's after hours or right after class 짯짯 or right after the work, it's compensable. There are a whole series of 짯짯

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Unless it's covered by the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. And the Portal짯to짯 Portal Act is an exception to that.

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN
turns.text: No. The regulations point blank say it's covered. There's no Portal짯to짯Portal Act argument on those regulations.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The notion that if it is compensable work it's covered, there is an exception to 짯짯 there's an exception to what the employer requires. He can require some things and yet not be liable for, under 짯짯 under the Act, to pay for those things. That's what the Portal짯to짯Portal Act is about.

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN
turns.text: The 짯짯 and we say those exceptions that he's not required to pay for are the transportation to and from the mine, which is (1)(a) 짯짯 or (a)(1) of the Portal 짯짯 254(a)(1), and then you don't get to (a)(2) if it's a 짯짯 if it's a principal activity. And the regulations on attending seminars does not discuss any exemption about way in or way out. It simply says that if the employer mandates you take a seminar, no matter what it's on, they have to pay you for the time. So there's no Portal짯to짯Portal Act issue if it's a principal activity. And what the regulations of 78 짯짯 785 say, these are lists of things that are compensable because the employer told you to do it. And it's 짯짯 whether it's fueling the truck at the end of the day 짯짯 you don't need to have the truck fueled at the end of the day because you're already done driving, so it's not integral and indispensable to anything. Those are the kind of things that the Portal짯to짯Portal Act doesn't cover because they're principal activities. I 짯짯 I could 짯짯 I could 짯짯 sexual harassment training, another one. We require employees to go through sexual harassment training. Is that a part of their job? It's a task. It's required of them.

turns.text: Well, the theory, I think 짯짯 I 짯짯 I think that in 1947, that's what they 짯짯 they thought it was such a 짯짯 I don't want to use the word "de minimis" because it has whole other meaning, but it's 짯짯

I mean, you don't. You don't check out twice. And the difference between checking in 짯짯 because, like, I don't have a checkout example 짯짯 but the difference in checking in and a roll call 짯짯 and 짯짯 and you must get paid for roll calls 짯짯 is that the employer tells you to be there at a certain time and do this thing where 짯짯 and that's compensable. But if a 짯짯 so if you guild or if you put the one straw on top of the checking out, you break the camel. And 짯짯 and it's a very, very small exception, because generally speaking, we want people not to work off the clock, because this is what this case is all about. It's about 짯짯

turns.name: MARK R. THIERMAN

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Mr. Clement you have four minutes.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice. Just a few points in rebuttal. I'd like to start with what is the linchpin of principal activities. Principal activities is obviously a term introduced to the Act by the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. The regulations define that as work of consequence. Now, my friend would like you to say that the linchpin is whether it's required. Here's why that makes no sense at all. Whether you get into the Fair Labor Standards Act in the first instance is determined by whether it's required. Now, it doesn't much 짯짯 do much good for Congress to say, don't worry about that, employers. We have an exception for you. But the exception is also never satisfied as long as it's required. That would give the exception no 짯짯

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: That isn't really, I think, their main point. I think this is 짯짯 this isn't really like they say, just checking out. What it is more like is drug testing. You heard the argument. Drug testing 짯짯

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: I heard their argument.

turns.name: STEPHEN BREYER
turns.text: 짯짯 a seminar that you'd have on the thing about sexual harassment, da, da, da. Okay. So what is your response?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Especially as my friend describes drug testing, it doesn't sound like any logical part of the egress process. And I think that's why those things are not covered. They're not covered not because 짯짯

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But you, certainly, Mr. Clement, make it part of the egress process. I mean, if this is just ingress/egress and I 짯짯 I 짯짯 seems as though that's part of what the Portal짯to짯Portal Act does. Why not make it part of the egress process and then there would be a wholly different outcome; is that right?

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: Well, no, I don't think so. I think at some point, the Court can obviously take 짯짯 police employers that want to lard on things that have nothing to do with the egress process and say you've got a wash my car on the way out. And as he describes the drug testing process, you're actually diverted from your process of exiting. You're escorted 짯짯 escorted to 짯짯 to the restroom and monitored while you do it. It doesn't sound anything like an egress process. And those activities are covered not because they are postliminary activities that are integral and indispensable. Those activities are covered because they're work under the FLSA and the portal짯to짯portal exceptions for postliminary and preliminary activity simply don't apply. And that covers car washing; it covers drug testing; it covers also these videos. It really is quite different. But the checkout process, which is part of the egress process, really does make this very close. One other point of clarification on this, Justice Scalia, you were exactly right when you said the 785 series, which they want to rely on, are about hours worked under the Fair Labor Standards Act. They say what's in the universe of things that are potentially subject to the exceptions of the Portal짯to짯Portal Act. The principal activities is defined in the regulations in the 790 series, which is all about the Portal짯to짯Portal Act and 790.A is helpfully entitled principal activities. What are principal activities? Not anything that the employer requires, but work of consequence and that gets you to this concept that work of consequence is things like cutting grapefruit or preparing every work station in the facility or driving the bus that gets people to the mine. Work of consequence is not checking in and checking out. It is not going through security. Nobody gets paid to go through security all day. Now, it also 짯짯 nobody gets paid take a bath all day either. And so that's why 짯짯 that's not a complete test. You still have to ask the final question, which is whether it's integral and indispensable to the principal activities. Now, my friends really don't want to talk about that, I think, with good reason because this clearly is not integral and indispensable. And I don't think you can make not stealing indispensable to the workday; otherwise, all these employees could stay home away from the 짯짯 the merchandise and they'd never steal a thing and that would be their work function. It just doesn't work. The work function is to fulfill the orders. I want to say one last thing about your opening hypothetical, Justice Kagan. There is another difference between your hypothetical and this situation, which at least as I understood your hypothetical, if an employee has to stay at their work station and while a 20짯minute process goes on, that's the employer requiring them to be on the premises for an extra 20 minutes. That's much more like being engaged to wait than waiting to engage.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I gave it to you two ways. And one was at your work station and the other was an employer, who knowing that there was this ingress/egress rule, made it part of the ingress/egress process. So that's my other question repeated again.

turns.name: PAUL D. CLEMENT
turns.text: But 짯짯 but even as to the modified hypothetical, if you have to stay on the employer's premises for an extra 20 minutes, that's closer to being engaged to wait as opposed to waiting to engage. The allegations here are not that this process takes 25 minutes. It can take up to 25 minutes if you're in the very back of the line. And I think one of the many reasons not to adopt their rule is you don't want to create an incentive for every employee to try to get to the back of the line, which is hardly going to speed things up.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
